agent_output: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a managed queue) is the clear recommendation — but scoped to a lightweight implementation using a managed service like SQS or Cloud Tasks, not a full Kafka/RabbitMQ deployment. The latency tolerance, reliability requirement, and future channel extensibility all point toward event-driven, while the small team constraint demands we pick managed infrastructure over self-hosted. Option A (synchronous REST) would be simpler initially but creates a brittle, tightly-coupled system that violates the reliability requirement and makes adding channels painful.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle. Emits domain events (DeliveryStatusChanged) when status transitions occur. Has zero knowledge of notification channels.","boundaries":"Bounded by delivery domain. Does not know about email, SMS, or any notification channel. Publishes events to a queue and moves on."},{"name":"Event Queue (Managed)","responsibility":"Durable, at-least-once message delivery. Decouples producers from consumers. Provides retry with backoff and dead-letter queue (DLQ) for failed messages.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern — use a managed service (AWS SQS, GCP Cloud Tasks, or equivalent). Not a custom broker."},{"name":"Notification Worker","responsibility":"Consumes domain events from the queue. Resolves customer notification preferences. Dispatches to the appropriate channel adapters. Handles per-channel retry logic.","boundaries":"Owns the mapping from domain events to notification actions. Does NOT own delivery domain logic."},{"name":"Channel Adapters","responsibility":"Thin wrappers around external provider APIs (SendGrid, Twilio, etc.). Each adapter implements a common interface: send(recipient, message) → Result. Handles provider-specific serialization and error mapping.","boundaries":"One adapter per channel. No business logic — pure integration. Swappable without touching the worker."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue + Alerting","responsibility":"Captures messages that exhaust all retries. Triggers an alert (PagerDuty, Slack webhook) so a human investigates. Prevents silent message loss.","boundaries":"Operational concern. Messages in the DLQ are anomalies, not normal flow."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Queue","contract":"Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged event: {deliveryId, status, customerId, timestamp, metadata}","coupling_type":"loose — fire-and-forget via queue"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Notification Worker","contract":"Worker polls/receives messages. At-least-once delivery. Worker must be idempotent.","coupling_type":"loose — async consumption"},{"from":"Notification Worker","to":"Channel Adapters","contract":"NotificationChannel interface: send(recipient: Contact, message: NotificationPayload) → Result<Success, ChannelError>","coupling_type":"loose — polymorphic dispatch via interface"},{"from":"Channel Adapters","to":"External Providers","contract":"Provider-specific REST/SDK calls (SendGrid API, Twilio API, etc.)","coupling_type":"tight to specific provider — isolated behind adapter boundary"}],"data_flow":"Delivery Service transitions a delivery status → emits DeliveryStatusChanged event to managed queue → Notification Worker picks up event → resolves customer preferences (email? SMS? both?) → calls appropriate Channel Adapter(s) → adapter calls external provider → on success, message is acknowledged and removed from queue → on failure, message returns to queue for retry with exponential backoff → after max retries, message moves to DLQ and alert fires.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (lightweight)","rationale":"The 5-minute latency tolerance makes async natural. Decouples the delivery domain from notification concerns entirely. A failed notification never blocks delivery processing."},{"pattern":"Strategy Pattern (Channel Adapters)","rationale":"Each notification channel implements a common interface. Adding WhatsApp or push notifications means adding a new adapter — zero changes to existing code. This is the primary flexibility point."},{"pattern":"At-Least-Once Delivery + Idempotency","rationale":"The reliability requirement ('never silently drop') maps directly to at-least-once semantics with idempotent consumers. Cheaper and simpler than exactly-once, and duplicate notifications are far less harmful than lost ones."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Provides a safety net for the reliability requirement. Messages that can't be processed after N retries are captured, not discarded. Paired with alerting, this closes the gap between 'automatic retry' and 'human intervention'."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer (Channel Adapters)","rationale":"External provider APIs change independently. The adapter boundary prevents Twilio's or SendGrid's API quirks from leaking into the notification worker's logic."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, tracks status lifecycle, emits domain events on transitions)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks dispatch status per channel, ensures idempotency)","CustomerPreferences (root: customerId, stores channel preferences and contact info)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":[]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Delivery defines the event schema. Notification conforms to it but owns how events are translated into notifications."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Open Host Service — Notification exposes a clean interface that channel adapters implement. Each adapter is a conformist to its external provider's API."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B (Event-Driven) over Option A (Synchronous REST)","gains":"Reliability via durable queues and automatic retry. Clean separation of delivery and notification concerns. Adding channels requires only a new adapter, no changes to delivery service. Failed notifications never block delivery processing. Natural backpressure handling.","sacrifices":"Slightly more moving parts (queue + worker). Requires understanding async patterns. Debugging is harder than following a synchronous call stack. Need to handle idempotency explicitly.","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST — simpler initially, but creates tight coupling between delivery and notification. Every new channel adds another synchronous call to the delivery service's critical path. A slow email provider degrades delivery processing. Retry logic must be hand-rolled in the delivery service, mixing concerns. A single provider timeout can cascade. For 3 engineers, this technical debt compounds fast."]},{"decision":"Managed Queue (SQS/Cloud Tasks) over self-hosted broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka)","gains":"Zero operational burden for the queue itself. Built-in retry, DLQ, monitoring. The 3-person team focuses on business logic, not broker uptime. Scales automatically.","sacrifices":"Vendor lock-in to cloud provider. Less control over routing semantics. Slightly higher per-message cost at scale (irrelevant for notification volumes).","alternatives_considered":["Self-hosted RabbitMQ — powerful but requires operational expertise the team doesn't have. Broker failure = total notification outage. Not justified for this scale.","Redis Streams — lighter weight but lacks built-in DLQ and retry semantics. Would require custom reliability logic, defeating the purpose."]},{"decision":"At-least-once delivery over exactly-once","gains":"Dramatically simpler implementation. No distributed transaction coordination. Managed queues support this natively.","sacrifices":"Duplicate notifications are possible (customer might receive the same SMS twice). Requires idempotency keys to minimize this.","alternatives_considered":["Exactly-once — requires transactional outbox pattern or two-phase commit. Massive complexity increase for a problem (duplicate notification) that is annoying but not business-critical."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, push, in-app) are added by implementing the NotificationChannel interface and deploying a new adapter. No changes to the delivery service, queue, or worker routing logic beyond a config/preference update."},{"area":"Notification Providers","evolution_path":"Switching from SendGrid to Mailgun means swapping one adapter implementation. The worker and all other adapters are untouched."},{"area":"Notification Logic","evolution_path":"Adding features like batching, digest emails, quiet hours, or priority levels happens entirely within the Notification bounded context. The Delivery context is unaffected."},{"area":"Event Schema Evolution","evolution_path":"New fields can be added to DeliveryStatusChanged with backward compatibility. Workers ignore unknown fields. Breaking changes use a new event version with a migration period."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"DeliveryStatusChanged Event Schema","reason":"This is the contract between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changes require coordination. This is intentional — the event schema should be stable because it represents a genuine domain concept (a delivery changed status). Rigidity here prevents accidental coupling."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Semantics","reason":"The system assumes idempotent consumers. All workers and adapters must be designed with this constraint. Switching to exactly-once later would be a significant rearchitecture."},{"area":"Queue as Single Integration Point","reason":"All notifications flow through one queue. This is a deliberate chokepoint for reliability and observability, but it means the queue service is a hard dependency."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Queue service outage (even managed services have downtime)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Delivery service should have a small local buffer or circuit breaker that holds events briefly during queue unavailability. Managed services have 99.9%+ SLA, so this is rare but must be handled. Alternatively, the Delivery service can persist events to its own database first (transactional outbox) and relay them to the queue, guaranteeing no event loss even during queue downtime."},{"risk":"Idempotency failures leading to duplicate notifications","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a deduplication store (simple database table with notificationId + channel as unique key). Check before dispatching. Accept that occasional duplicates are tolerable — a customer getting two 'your package arrived' SMS messages is annoying but not harmful."},{"risk":"External provider rate limiting or outage","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Channel adapters implement exponential backoff with jitter. Circuit breaker pattern per provider. DLQ captures messages that exhaust retries. Alerting ensures human intervention for prolonged outages."},{"risk":"Team unfamiliarity with async patterns","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Start with the simplest possible queue setup (SQS with default settings, single worker). Add complexity (concurrent workers, batching) only when needed. Document the message flow with a clear diagram. The 5-minute latency tolerance means there's no pressure to optimize throughput early."},{"risk":"Message ordering not guaranteed","severity":"low","mitigation":"Notification messages are independent — order doesn't matter. A customer receiving 'out for delivery' after 'delivered' is confusing but can be handled by including timestamps in the notification payload and letting the client/template show the correct state."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Deploy a managed queue (SQS or equivalent). Add event publishing to the Delivery service at status transition points. Build one Notification Worker with two Channel Adapters (email via SendGrid, SMS via Twilio). Configure DLQ with max 3 retries and exponential backoff. Add a Slack webhook alert for DLQ arrivals. Phase 2 (when needed): Add customer notification preferences. Add new channel adapters. Add idempotency deduplication table. Phase 3 (when scale demands): Add concurrent workers. Add batching for high-volume events.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/DeliveryStatusChanged.ts — domain event definition","src/delivery/services/DeliveryService.ts — emit events on status transitions","src/notification/worker/NotificationWorker.ts — queue consumer, routes to adapters","src/notification/channels/NotificationChannel.ts — interface definition","src/notification/channels/EmailAdapter.ts — SendGrid integration","src/notification/channels/SmsAdapter.ts — Twilio integration","src/notification/templates/TemplateResolver.ts — maps event type + channel to message template","infra/queue.tf or cdk/queue.ts — IaC for managed queue + DLQ","src/notification/idempotency/DeduplicationStore.ts — prevents duplicate sends"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a simple interface for channels (send method returning a Result type). Keep adapters thin — no business logic. Worker should be a pure function: receive event → resolve preferences → dispatch to adapters → ack. Use structured logging with correlationId (deliveryId) through the entire flow for debuggability.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build a custom message broker — use managed infrastructure","Do NOT put notification logic in the Delivery service — keep contexts separate","Do NOT retry indefinitely — set a max retry count and use DLQ","Do NOT ignore idempotency — at-least-once means duplicates WILL happen","Do NOT over-engineer the first version — start with 2 channels, add complexity when proven necessary","Do NOT use synchronous calls from Delivery to Notification 'just for now' — the async boundary is the most important architectural decision and retrofitting it later is painful"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Event-driven over synchronous is clearly correct given the stated requirements (reliability, extensibility, latency tolerance, small team)","Managed queue over self-hosted is clearly correct for a 3-person team","Strategy pattern for channel adapters is the standard, proven approach","At-least-once over exactly-once is the right trade-off for notifications"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on the team's cloud provider, which is unknown","Whether the team has existing async/event-driven experience affects the implementation timeline","Customer preference storage — unclear whether this exists already or needs to be built"],"assumptions":["The team uses a major cloud provider (AWS, GCP, or Azure) that offers a managed queue service","Notification volume is moderate (thousands per day, not millions) — typical for a courier company","The delivery service already has a database and can be modified to emit events","Email and SMS are the immediate channels; push and WhatsApp are future requirements"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a managed queue) is the clear recommendation — but scoped to a lightweight implementation using a managed service like SQS or Cloud Tasks, not a full Kafka/RabbitMQ deployment.
